course_description:
Marine wildlife (marine mammals, reptiles, sharks and rays) is threatened by extinction and faces compounding pressures from human activities including over-exploitation, pollution, coastal development and climate change. There is an urgent need to understand and manage these threats. In this course, you will examine the major conservation issues facing threatened marine wildlife globally with a particular focus on Australia. You will learn how marine wildlife research, management and conservation is conducted using ecological, population and life history data, and how threats may be managed. Teaching will be conducted through a series of lectures, on-line SDL modules, specialist workshops with industry partners and researchers, field exercises and small group projects undertaken in one of the most diverse marine wildlife locations in Australia, the subtropical waters of the Moreton Bay region.

This course will improve graduate employability through a work-integrated learning approach in which you will develop key field skills. These will include wildlife survey techniques including species ID, land-based tracking, aerial photogrammetry and acoustics, boat-based data collection, data analysis and wildlife necropsy.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
We recommend completing the following courses before enrolling in this one:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
There are two optional (but highly recommended) field trips associated with this course:
Sat 9 - Sun 10 August 2025: 2-day field trip to the university's research station at Dunwich, North Stradbroke Island. ($160 includes return travel from UQ, accommodation, food and activities)
Sat 13 September 2025: full day excursion to Sea World, Gold Coast for a behind-the-scenes tour. ($60 includes entry to Sea World and return travel from UQ OR $45 for entry to Sea World with student providing own transport)
course_aims: To examine the major conservation issues facing threatened marine wildlife globally and in Australia. Be exposed to how marine wildlife research, management and conservation is conducted using ecological, population and life history data, and how threats may be managed.

learning_outcomes.description: Based on a comprehensive knowledge of the biology and habitat requirements of a range of marine wildlife, evaluate the range of methodological approaches to collect, analyse and interpret critical field data for marine wildlife conservation.
Appraise and critique diverse approaches for evaluating conservation status of marine wildlife on regional and global scales.
Formulate appropriate research and conservation priorities for threatened marine wildlife.
Demonstrate competencies in standard field skills for working with marine wildlife including conducting surveys, recording behaviours, biological sampling, and evaluating habitat .
Demonstrate competencies in design and conduct of research projects in the field including data collection, analysis and communication.

assessment_details.task_description: Three quizzes to test modules 2 - 4, each worth 10%. 1) Quiz (module 2 quiz) opens on Friday 5 September 2025 2) Quiz (module 3 quiz) opens on Friday 10 October 2025 3) Quiz (module 4 quiz) opens on Friday 14 November 2025 Each quiz will have to be completed by the specified due date. assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Online submission via Inspera only by the due date and time.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
See the Additional assessment information section below for information relating to extension applications.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

learning_activities.topic: NO WORKSHOP No workshop today but SDL#5 is available to students
Sea World Excursion Saturday 13 September 2025, date to be confirmed Behind the scenes day trip to Sea World, Gold Coast Lecture: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Lecture 23: Emerging threats to marine wildlife Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Lecture 1: Introduction Course structure, house-keeping; marine wildlife definitions Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Lecture 2: Conservation status Conservation status and protection of marine mammals, reptiles, seabirds. Drivers and hotspots of extinction risk in marine wildlife Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Workshop #1: Introduction Overview of course: assessment items including 2 assignments, self directed learning (SDL) sessions and quizzes, Workshops: (i) skill development, (ii) panel discussion Qs. SDL#1 & #2 available to students. Lectures: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L02, L03
Lecture 3: Marine mammal behaviour An overview of marine mammal behaviour Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Lecture 4: Impacts of historical exploitation on species, populations Effects of historical exploitation (commercial, subsistence harvest) on present day conservation status of marine wildlife. Case studies include great whales globally, Southern Ocean pinnipeds. Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01
NO WORKSHOP THIS WEEK No workshop this week BUT SDL#3 is available to students

FIELD TRIP TO MORETON BAY 8 - 9 AUGUST 2024 Field trip to North Stradbroke Island / Moreton Bay: humpback whale surveys, dugong genetic sampling, seabird survey techniques. Staff: Janet Lanyon, Rebecca Dunlop / Michael Noad, tutors Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Lecture 5: Population size & trends Population size & trends, reproductive capacity including case studies. Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Lecture 6: Case study: Humpback whales Case study: Population study of East Australian humpback whales Lecturer: Mike Noad Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Workshop #2 Population disturbance Population Consequences of Disturbance workshop SDL#4 available to students Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Lecture 7: Habitat requirements Habitat requirements and utilisation including foraging and movements, and threats, including case studies Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Lecture 8: Anthropogenic ocean noise as a threat Anthropogenic ocean noise as a threat to marine animal habitats: interviews from Ocean Noise conference, Berlin July 2022 Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Lecture 9: Acoustic survey methods & tracking Acoustic survey methods & tracking studies Lecturer: Mike Noad Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Lecture 10: Visual survey techniques Visual survey techniques including line transect, strip transects, migration counts, photo ID; survey platforms (aerial, boat, land), cues for species ID Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Workshop #3: Marine acoustics Marine acoustics: recording & analysis of cetacean calls SDL#6 available to students Lecturer: Mike Noad Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05

Lecture 11: Putting it all together #1: Humpback whales Putting it all together #1: behavioural observations, acoustic surveys, tracking, experiments. Case study = BRAHSS expt. Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Lecture 12: Sampling without capture #1 Sampling without capture: necropsy of carcasses, live strandings, visual observations (survey, behavioural obs) Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Workshop #4: Distance sampling; Quiz #1 opens Distance sampling using visual survey data Lecturer: Rebecca Dunlop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Lecture 13: Sampling without capture #2 Biopsy techniques without capture, using tracks and signs Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Lecture 14: Capture and sampling methods Capture techniques, sampling after capture, health assessment, captivity Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04

Workshop #5: NECROPSY OF MARINE WILDLIFE Necropsy of Marine Wildlife workshop: identification and carcass necropsy, biological data collection in lab 8-305 SDL#7 available to students Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Lecture 15: Putting it all together #2 Putting it all together #2: Case study = Moreton Bay dugongs Part 1 feeding/habitat requirements, genetics. Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Lecture 16: Putting it all together #2 ctd Putting it all together #2: Case study = Moreton Bay dugongs Part 2- endocrinology, physiology, health. Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Workshop #6: Lecture ctd + Research Tools assignment background; Assignment #1 due This workshop session will consist of Putting it all Together #2, then background for the Research Tools assignment, i.e., appropriate research approaches to address research questions & conservation needs, including when resources are scarce: kicking off Assignment #2 Assignment #1 due today Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Lecture 17: What should we conserve? Species/populations to conserve? Decisions, priorities, economic value & triage, conservation tools. Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Workshop #7: ROLES OF RESCUE, REHAB AND RESEARCH IN HUMAN CARE; Quiz #2 opens The roles of marine wildlife research, rescue and rehab, breeding, public display and education in managed facilities Guest lecturer Dr Claire Madden. Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Lecture 18: Seabird conservation 1 Lecturer: Elize Ng Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Lecture 19: Seabird conservation 2 Lecturer: Elize Ng Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Workshop #8: Climate change and sea turtles Climate change and sea turtles: conservation strategies Guest lecturer: David Booth Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

Lecture 20: Humpback Whale Sentinel Program Surveillance of chemicals and climate change in Antarctica Lecturer: Susan Bengtson-Nash: Note: this is a recorded lecture Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Lecture 21: Shark conservation Lecturer: Dr Christine Dudgeon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

Workshop #9: Elasmobranchs: roles of ecotourism and citizen science in conservation; Assignment #2 due The role of ecotourism in marine wildlife conservation. Case study = Elasmobranchs and tourism, citizen science in action Guest lecturer: Dr Christine Dudgeon Lecturer: Janet Lanyon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Lecture 22: Microplastics and marine wildlife Lecturer: Amanda Dawson Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

Workshop #10: Protected areas and marine wildlife conservation How to define critical habitat for marine wildlife (including IMMAs Important Marine Mammal Areas, MPAs Marine Protected Areas, BIAs), and conservation at the global level Guest Lecturer: Justin Meager Lecturers: Lanyon, Noad Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
